name: Maui Full
description: Maui is a demigod and trickster figure prominent in Polynesian mythology. Born prematurely and cast into the sea by his mother, Taranga, he was rescued and raised by his grandfather, Tama-nui-i-te-ra. Maui is known for his extraordinary feats of strength and cunning, which he used to benefit humanity. He is credited with slowing the sun's passage across the sky, fishing up islands from the ocean depths, discovering the secret of fire, and attempting to conquer death. His powers include shapeshifting, superhuman strength, and the ability to manipulate natural forces. Maui's role is that of a cultural hero, a trickster, and a benefactor of humankind. He is revered for his ingenuity and his daring exploits, which brought light, land, and essential resources to the Polynesian people. His stories are filled with humor and adventure, showcasing his complex character and his enduring legacy. Despite his heroic deeds, Maui's hubris often leads to his downfall, reminding mortals of their limitations. He is a central figure in Polynesian cosmology, embodying both the potential and the perils of challenging the natural order. Maui's actions continue to shape the world and inspire generations.
subtitle: The Polynesian Trickster and Demigod

keyMyths.title: Maui Slows the Sun
keyMyths.description: Maui noticed the days were too short, hindering work. He trapped the sun with ropes and beat it until it agreed to move slower across the sky.

keyMyths.title: Maui Fishes Up Islands
keyMyths.description: Using his magical fishhook, Maui pulled up islands from the depths of the ocean. These islands became the lands inhabited by the Polynesian people.

keyMyths.title: Maui Discovers Fire
keyMyths.description: Maui tricked the mudhens into revealing the secret of fire. He then shared this knowledge with humanity, allowing them to cook and stay warm.

keyMyths.title: Maui Tries to Conquer Death
keyMyths.description: Maui attempted to enter the body of Hine-nui-te-po, the goddess of night, to reverse the process of death for humankind. He failed when a fantail laughed and woke the goddess, who crushed him.

sources.author: Sir George Grey

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of First Fruits
worshipPractices.description: In some Polynesian cultures, the first fruits of harvests or catches were offered to Maui as a sign of respect and gratitude for his role in providing sustenance.

worshipPractices.name: Chants and Prayers
worshipPractices.description: Specific chants and prayers were sometimes dedicated to Maui, recounting his heroic deeds and seeking his blessings for strength, cunning, or success in endeavors.

festivals.name: Maui-tikitiki-a-Taranga Celebrations
festivals.description: In some regions, specific days or periods might be dedicated to celebrating Maui's accomplishments through storytelling, dance, and feasting.

cultural.modernLegacy: Maui remains a prominent figure in Polynesian identity and pride. His stories are retold in schools, cultural performances, and contemporary art, inspiring creativity, resilience, and a connection to ancestral heritage. He is also featured in modern media, such as the Disney movie 'Moana', introducing his stories to a global audience, though often with significant creative license.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Maui's birth is shrouded in myth and mystery across Polynesia, but a common thread persists: his mother, Taranga, discarded him shortly after birth. In some versions, he was stillborn or prematurely born. Cast into the ocean, he was miraculously saved, often by divine intervention or the care of ancestral spirits. In many accounts, his grandfather, Tama-nui-i-te-ra, the sun, plays a pivotal role in his upbringing, nurturing him and bestowing upon him extraordinary powers. This abandonment and miraculous survival sets the stage for Maui's heroic journey, highlighting his resilience and connection to the supernatural realm. His unusual birth marks him as different, destined for greatness and challenges, setting him apart from ordinary mortals and foreshadowing his role as a cultural hero and trickster.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Maui's mythology is rich with tales of his audacious feats. One of his most famous accomplishments is slowing the sun's passage across the sky. Frustrated by the short days that left little time for work, Maui snared the sun with ropes woven from his sister's hair, forcing it to travel more slowly, thus lengthening the days. Another significant legend recounts Maui fishing up islands from the depths of the ocean using a magical fishhook (Manaia). These islands form the Polynesian archipelago. He is also credited with discovering the secret of fire, battling monstrous creatures, and attempting to conquer death for humanity. These stories highlight Maui's resourcefulness, his willingness to challenge the natural order, and his dedication to improving the lives of his people, even if it means employing trickery and bending the rules.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: While not always the central figure of formal worship, Maui held a significant place of reverence in Polynesian societies. He was often invoked in prayers and chants, especially concerning matters of strength, cunning, and success in difficult tasks. Offerings of first fruits and catches were sometimes made to honor his role in providing sustenance. Specific cult practices varied across different islands and tribal groups, reflecting the diverse interpretations of his mythology. He was seen as a cultural hero and ancestor, embodying the qualities of ingenuity, bravery, and a willingness to challenge the status quo. His stories served as moral lessons and entertainment, reinforcing cultural values and inspiring future generations. The respect for Maui was woven into the fabric of daily life, rather than confined to specific temples or rituals.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Maui's legacy extends far beyond ancient myths. He remains a potent symbol of Polynesian identity, ingenuity, and cultural pride. His stories continue to be retold through oral traditions, dance, and artistic expressions, ensuring his enduring presence in the collective memory. In modern times, Maui's character has been reinterpreted and adapted in various forms of media, including literature, film, and animation, introducing his tales to a global audience. While these adaptations often take creative liberties, they contribute to the ongoing evolution of his mythology and solidify his status as a timeless cultural icon. Maui's influence can also be seen in contemporary movements promoting cultural preservation and empowerment within Polynesian communities, inspiring a renewed appreciation for ancestral knowledge and traditions.

powers: Control over the Elements (in some stories)
shortDescription: A Polynesian demigod and trickster, Maui is renowned for his incredible strength and cunning, performing feats to benefit humanity.
